questions.question: What must a true beginner understand and practice before independently creating valid meaningful HTML documents?
acceptanceEvidence: Current standards and beginner curriculum authorities map to explicit explanations, worked examples, early editing, faded practice, debugging, and changed-case assessment.

questions.question: Which current HTML, CSS, accessibility, responsive-design, and certification objectives require original teaching and practice?
acceptanceEvidence: Every versioned external objective maps to an original competency and complete introduce-through-transfer learning sequence.

questions.question: Which inline editing, preview, diagnostics, testing, recovery, and evidence features let learners build and verify web artifacts accessibly?
acceptanceEvidence: Tablet and desktop learner tasks pass through the one CodeMirror 6 editor path with sandboxed preview, semantic diagnostics, changed-case checks, persistence, recovery, and keyboard, touch, or assistive-technology use.

intendedLearners: True beginners and returning learners who need a complete path from their first HTML element to independently designed accessible responsive sites.
entryCompetencies: Use keyboard or equivalent input, open a supported browser, and follow accessible file and navigation instructions.
terminalTasks: Design, build, test, and defend an original multi-page responsive website for an unfamiliar stakeholder and varied content.
Diagnose semantic, layout, accessibility, responsive, performance, and interaction defects from browser evidence and verify durable repairs.
Explain and justify HTML structure, CSS architecture, responsive choices, accessible behavior, and tradeoffs without relying on copied workshop recipes.
scopeIncludes: Computer, file, browser, URL, source, DOM, accessibility-tree, layout, and developer-tool foundations introduced only where needed for immediate building

HTML syntax, elements, content, nesting, attributes, documents, metadata, text semantics, landmarks, links, images, media ownership and licensing, lists, tables, forms, validation, and native interaction
CSS syntax, cascade, inheritance, specificity, selectors, values, units, box model, typography, color, backgrounds, borders, effects, layout, positioning, Flexbox, Grid, and custom properties
Intrinsic and responsive design, media and container queries, responsive media and typography, preferences, motion, accessibility, debugging, testing, and maintainability
Original workshops, faded practice, debugging clinics, independent labs, reviews, quizzes, five certification projects, delayed assessment, and portfolio defense
scopeExcludes: Framework-specific component systems, production backend services, and JavaScript application architecture beyond bounded native web behavior
authenticTasks: Build an accessible public information site whose semantic structure remains useful before CSS and across assistive technology.
Build and test a data-collection experience with clear labels, instructions, validation, error recovery, and responsive layout.
Create a content-heavy reference site that withstands long text, localization, zoom, forced colors, missing resources, and varied viewports.
Diagnose a broken responsive interface using source, DOM, computed-style, layout, accessibility, network, and console evidence.
Defend an unfamiliar stakeholder site against semantic, accessibility, responsive, performance, and maintainability acceptance criteria.
knownMisconceptions: HTML is visual formatting text rather than a parsed structure that communicates meaning and relationships.
A tag name alone proves semantic correctness regardless of content model, nesting, accessible name, relationship, or context.
Responsive design means selecting a few device widths instead of building content-driven resilient systems.
Passing a source substring check proves rendered structure, behavior, accessibility, and changed cases work.
Accessibility can be added after visual completion without changing structure, content, interaction, or testing.
safetyBoundaries: Learner HTML, CSS, and JavaScript render only in an opaque sandboxed preview with bounded navigation, output, and no same-origin access.
Canonical hidden checks and solutions remain server-side; learner submissions are evidence rather than answer authority.
Projects use fictional or learner-owned content and never require unsafe publication, credentials, tracking, external contact, or unauthorized assets.

claims.statement: HTML syntax, elements, content models, document conformance, forms, media, tables, and author requirements control technical correctness.
limitations: Living Standard is authoritative technical scope but is not a novice teaching sequence or evidence that a learner can apply requirements.

triggers: WHATWG changes an element, content model, conformance rule, form behavior, or API used in course material.

claims.statement: CSS Snapshot identifies the stable CSS specification surface while linked modules control detailed syntax and behavior.
limitations: Snapshot status does not establish browser interoperability, beginner sequence, accessibility, or project usefulness for each feature.

triggers: W3C publishes a newer CSS Snapshot or changes status of a feature taught as stable.

claims.statement: CSS stylesheets contain qualified rules and at-rules; style rules bind selectors to declaration blocks, and parsing has defined recovery behavior rather than treating every malformed token as a whole-stylesheet failure.
limitations: The editor's draft is work in progress and defines syntax and parsing, not property semantics, browser interoperability, novice sequence, or evidence of learner mastery.

triggers: CSSWG changes rule, declaration, at-rule, parsing, error-recovery, or conformance text used by the course.

claims.statement: Selectors match elements through type, class, ID, attribute, relationship, state, and functional forms; specificity compares ID, class-like, and type-like columns with special rules for functional pseudo-classes rather than a decimal or four-place score.
limitations: The editor's draft is work in progress, identifies at-risk features, and does not by itself establish interoperable support, accessible interaction, teaching order, or assessment validity.

triggers: CSSWG changes selector syntax, matching, specificity, pseudo-class, pseudo-element, privacy, or at-risk status used by the course.

sources.title: CSS Cascading and Inheritance Level 5
sources.versionOrPublished: W3C Candidate Recommendation Snapshot 13 January 2022, current controlling level reviewed 2026-07-15

claims.statement: The cascade resolves declarations through relevance, origin and importance, encapsulation context, style attributes, layers, specificity, scope, and order; inheritance and the CSS-wide keywords remain distinct value processes.
limitations: Candidate Recommendation status and normative algorithms do not establish novice explanations, browser interoperability for every feature, maintainable author architecture, or learner transfer.

triggers: CSSWG advances a newer controlling cascade level or changes origin, importance, layers, defaulting, inheritance, or value-processing rules used by the course.

sources.title: CSS Cascading and Inheritance Module Level 6

claims.statement: Level 6 adds evolving scope behavior, but its own status directs implementers to Level 5; it is a freshness watch source rather than the controlling authority for core cascade instruction.
limitations: This is an exploratory diff specification, may change at any moment, and must not be presented as stable or used alone for implementation claims.

triggers: CSS Cascade Level 6 merges Level 5 text, advances maturity, or changes scope behavior relevant to the planned course.

claims.statement: The display property controls box-tree generation through outer and inner display types; legacy inline-block maps to inline flow-root, while changing CSS display does not change document semantics.
limitations: The Candidate Recommendation is work in progress, identifies at-risk features, and does not establish accessibility exposure or support for every display combination without browser and assistive-technology evidence.

triggers: CSSWG changes display decomposition, box generation, internal display, accessibility effects, or at-risk features taught by the course.

sources.title: CSS Backgrounds and Borders Module Level 3

claims.statement: Background layers, clipping and origin, border geometry and images, rounded corners, and box shadows have distinct paint and shorthand behavior tied to CSS box areas.
limitations: The Candidate Recommendation is work in progress and does not establish contrast, focus visibility, resilient fallback, interoperability, or instructional quality for learner artifacts.

triggers: CSSWG changes background, border, radius, shadow, shorthand, paint, or at-risk behavior taught by the course.

sources.title: CSS Values and Units Module Level 4

claims.statement: Percentages resolve against quantities defined by each accepting property; CSS lengths use distinct reference frames; default viewport units use the large viewport while small and dynamic variants have different stability tradeoffs; and calc arithmetic is valid only when numeric type algebra produces a value accepted by the property.
limitations: The Editor's Draft is work in progress, property modules still define percentage bases and accepted types, and specification validity does not prove interoperable browser behavior, accessible reflow, readable sizing, or learner transfer.

triggers: CSSWG changes percentages, absolute or font-relative lengths, viewport variants, reference pixels, calc grammar, numeric type algebra, zero handling, or module status.

claims.statement: Beginner CSS scope must include syntax, selectors, the box model, conflicts, values and units, sizing, backgrounds, borders, overflow, form styling, and evidence-led debugging.
limitations: Curriculum outcomes establish a bounded beginner scope but do not supply LEARN-IT-ALL activity sequencing, practice volume, grading validity, retention, or transfer evidence.

triggers: MDN changes CSS Fundamentals outcomes, resources, browser guidance, or debugging expectations.

claims.statement: Modern layout scope must begin with normal flow, use floats for their remaining content purpose, distinguish positioning models, and apply flexbox, grid, media queries, and viewport behavior to responsive systems.
limitations: The curriculum does not prove that one fixed device sequence, breakpoint set, or layout recipe transfers to unfamiliar content and user conditions.

triggers: MDN changes layout, flexbox, grid, positioning, media-query, viewport, or responsive-design outcomes.

claims.statement: Current applied CSS scope connects cascade, specificity, inheritance, logical properties, sizing, color, typography, layout, container queries, nesting, functions, focus, motion, and user preferences through computed browser behavior.
limitations: Official explanatory examples establish technical models but do not validate LEARN-IT-ALL prerequisites, independent performance, accessibility, or project originality.

triggers: web.dev changes Learn CSS scope, stable-feature guidance, examples, or browser-support claims.

claims.statement: Responsive work must adapt layout, navigation, images, typography, interaction, and testing to content and user conditions rather than target a short list of device widths.
limitations: Guidance and demonstrations cannot establish accessibility, performance, compatibility, or usability for a learner artifact without changed-content and supported-device testing.

triggers: web.dev changes responsive-layout, media, typography, interaction, or testing recommendations.

claims.statement: Perceivable, operable, understandable, and robust outcomes require semantic, keyboard, focus, status, reflow, contrast, target, input, and motion evidence.
limitations: Conformance does not prove overall usability, inclusive content quality, learner understanding, or compatibility with every assistive technology.

triggers: W3C publishes a superseding WCAG Recommendation or applicable normative correction.

claims.statement: Beginner front-end scope includes standards, semantic HTML, CSS, responsive design, accessibility, testing, browser knowledge, collaboration, and professional practice.
limitations: MDN supplies curriculum outcomes and learning resources but not this platform's complete interactive practice, assessment, correction, or credential evidence.

triggers: MDN changes curriculum modules, learning outcomes, assessment guidance, or Learn Web Development structure.

claims.statement: Beginner HTML needs an explicit model of documents as node trees containing elements, text, attributes, content, empty elements, and semantic categories.
limitations: Official explanatory material supports scope and model design but does not validate LEARN-IT-ALL sequence, practice, grading, accessibility, retention, or transfer.

triggers: web.dev changes Learn HTML scope, examples, browser guidance, or accessibility treatment.

claims.statement: Beginner browser understanding must connect navigation, HTTP requests and responses, resource discovery, HTML parsing, DOM construction, CSS matching, rendering, and the evidence exposed by developer tools.
limitations: The explanatory rendering model is intentionally simplified and cannot prove exact engine internals, network behavior, performance, compatibility, or learner understanding.

triggers: MDN changes its browser loading, parsing, DOM, CSS, render, request, response, or DevTools model.

sources.title: MDN Environment Setup for Beginning Web Developers

claims.statement: True-beginner web setup includes bounded operating-system, application, file, browser, and search competence, but those capabilities can enter around immediate web artifacts instead of delaying all HTML construction.
limitations: MDN assumes basic computer use and links to platform-specific help; it does not prove one universal setup flow, hardware requirement, support boundary, or learner readiness.

triggers: MDN changes beginner environment prerequisites, supported setup tasks, browser guidance, file guidance, or search sequence.

sources.title: MDN Installing Basic Web Development Software

claims.statement: Beginning web work needs a current editor and browser, while cross-browser evidence must distinguish browser brands, rendering engines, versions, update state, and authentic-device limits.
limitations: Product recommendations and platform availability can change; installing software also requires local authority and cannot be performed by the learner-code runtime.

triggers: MDN changes recommended editors, browser engines, update guidance, platform limits, local-server guidance, or tool sequencing.

claims.statement: An editor is a source and file tool with navigation, search, syntax, completion, formatting, diagnostics, and extensions; none of those capabilities alone proves browser behavior or learner mastery.
limitations: Editor features differ by product, extension, language, browser, device, and accessibility configuration and require direct verification in the selected workspace.

triggers: MDN changes code-editor capability, keyboard, extension, accessibility, file, search, formatting, or diagnostic guidance.

claims.statement: Portable beginner projects require safe learner-owned file operations, intentional extensions and names, one comprehensible project tree, keyboard access, and references tested after files move or change case.
limitations: File-manager interfaces and shortcuts vary by operating system and version; simulated operations cannot prove permissions, synchronization, recovery, or deployment behavior on every real device.

triggers: MDN changes file safety, naming, extensions, folder structure, paths, keyboard operation, editor trust, or platform-version guidance.

claims.statement: Beginners need observable distinctions among browser, page, website, server, address, URL, search query, search engine, result snippet, requested resource, parsed document, and rendered output.
limitations: The model is intentionally introductory; search results, browser interfaces, privacy behavior, ranking, and rendering details vary and require current direct observation.

triggers: MDN changes browser, website, search-engine, query, request, parsing, rendering, result-evaluation, or privacy guidance.

claims.statement: Internet access, internet service providers, the web, browsers, websites, servers, and search engines are distinct layers whose failure evidence should not be collapsed into a single connectivity claim.
limitations: The explainer is a simplified conceptual model and does not establish a specific provider topology, service guarantee, diagnostic authority, or complete networking course.

triggers: MDN changes its internet, ISP, client, server, web, browser, website, or search-engine model.

claims.statement: Safe sign-in instruction must prefer individual accounts and phishing-resistant multifactor authentication where available while explaining that passwords and weaker factors can still be captured or replayed.
limitations: Organizational identity guidance does not prescribe one consumer device workflow, remove recovery risk, or authorize learners to change real accounts during a web-design course.

triggers: CISA changes phishing-resistant MFA, password, recovery, authentication-factor, account, or implementation guidance.

sources.title: GOV.UK Service Manual User Needs and Research

claims.statement: User-centered interface decisions begin with evidence about varied users, outcomes, behavior, constraints, and barriers, then continue through prototypes, observation, validation, revision, and re-testing.
limitations: Government service guidance supplies a defensible research process but does not prescribe one visual style, prove a learner design, or replace accessibility standards and representative testing.

triggers: GOV.UK changes user-needs, research-planning, prototyping, observation, validation, or inclusive-participant guidance.

sources.title: MDN Progressive Enhancement
sources.versionOrPublished: Current glossary entry, last modified 2025-07-18; reviewed 2026-07-15

claims.statement: Progressive enhancement supplies essential content and functionality through a broadly usable baseline, then adds richer behavior where user-agent capability supports it while preserving accessibility alternatives.
limitations: The glossary defines the philosophy but does not prove which baseline is sufficient for a particular user task, feature, browser set, accessibility need, or failure condition.

triggers: MDN changes the baseline, feature-detection, accessibility-alternative, browser-capability, or progressive-enhancement guidance.

sources.title: CSS Color Module Level 4
sources.versionOrPublished: Editor's Draft 15 July 2026; latest published level and implementation report linked by the draft

claims.statement: CSS color values distinguish modern and legacy functional syntax, alpha, sRGB, perceptual and predefined spaces, conversion, interpolation, gamut mapping, resolved values, and serialization; valid authored color is not identical to guaranteed device output.
limitations: The Editor's Draft is work in progress, and specification text does not establish interoperable support, output-device gamut, calibrated appearance, contrast, accessibility, or an appropriate beginner sequence.

triggers: CSSWG changes color syntax, spaces, alpha, interpolation, gamut mapping, resolution, serialization, accessibility guidance, maturity, or implementation-report evidence.

claims.statement: CSS Color Level 5 defines evolving color derivation including color mixing and relative color syntax, with explicit origin, component, percentage, alpha, math, and interpolation-space rules.
limitations: The draft may change and includes features with different maturity and interoperability; every assessed function needs a current supported-browser decision and Web Platform Test or equivalent behavior evidence.

triggers: CSSWG changes color-mix, relative color syntax, contrast selection, device CMYK, grammar, processing, maturity, or test evidence.

claims.statement: Linear, radial, conic, and repeating gradients are generated images with defined geometry, stop fixup, interpolation methods, and sizing rather than flat color values or semantic content.
limitations: The draft contains evolving Level 4 behavior; specified interpolation defaults and advanced syntax cannot be treated as interoperable browser behavior without current implementation and changed-case evidence.

triggers: CSSWG changes gradient geometry, stop fixup, interpolation, sizing, syntax, maturity, or linked implementation tests.

claims.statement: Supported and preferred color schemes negotiate the used page and element schemes, affect browser-provided controls and system colors, and cannot safely repair arbitrary authored foreground and background combinations automatically.
limitations: The draft remains work in progress, user agents need not support every scheme, and declaring support does not prove palette contrast, readability, preference persistence, or usable controls.

triggers: CSSWG changes color-scheme, forced-colors, system-color, light-dark, print-color-adjust, or color-scheme negotiation behavior or status.

sources.title: CSS Basic User Interface Module Level 4
sources.versionOrPublished: W3C Working Draft 6 May 2026; Editor's Draft reviewed 2026-07-15

claims.statement: Focus outlines, accent-color, appearance, cursor, resize, caret, and other basic interface presentation affect operability and user-agent behavior. Accent colors can be adjusted by the user agent for contrast; appearance none suppresses native appearance and can leave a primitive or invisible control whose full contract must be restored by the author.
limitations: The specification does not make every form control equally stylable or prove an authored replacement accessible. The draft explicitly marks appearance base as not ready to ship, and current support, platform behavior, forced-color behavior, assistive-technology output, and input operation still require direct testing.

triggers: CSSWG changes outline, accent-color, appearance, cursor, resize, caret, control rendering, maturity, or implementation-test evidence.

sources.title: CSS Form Control Styling Level 1
sources.versionOrPublished: Editor's Draft 5 May 2026; freshness watch only

claims.statement: CSSWG is exploring more standardized form-control styling through appearance base, control pseudo-elements, field sizing, and related parts, but the draft contains open issues and incomplete sections and is not a production curriculum baseline.
limitations: This Editor's Draft warns that it is changing and should not be cited as anything other than work in progress. Draft syntax receives no assessed authoring requirement until maturity, supported-browser, Web Platform Test, accessibility, and failure evidence pass.

triggers: CSS Form Control Styling changes maturity, appearance base, pseudo-parts, field-sizing, accessibility requirements, implementation support, or test evidence.

claims.statement: CSS boxes have separate content, padding, border, and margin areas and edges. Backgrounds paint through defined inner areas, borders occupy and paint the border area, margins remain transparent, and edge keywords are reused by sizing, paint, clipping, and transform features.
limitations: Box-area definitions do not by themselves determine a useful size, spacing system, accessible target, responsive result, or beginner activity. Level 4 contains evolving features such as margin trimming that require separate maturity and support evidence.

triggers: CSSWG changes box areas, edges, margin or padding behavior, edge keywords, margin trimming, maturity, or linked implementation evidence.

claims.statement: Box sizing distinguishes the content and border sizing edges, applies to quantitative sizing properties, floors content size at zero, and can still produce a border box larger than the specified size when padding and border cannot fit. Some HTML controls use border-box behavior by default.
limitations: A border-box declaration does not guarantee content fits, prevent intrinsic minimum overflow, establish responsiveness, or prove used dimensions under changed content and zoom.

triggers: CSSWG changes sizing edges, box-sizing, intrinsic constraints, minimums, computed or used values, element defaults, maturity, or tests.

sources.title: Cascading Style Sheets Level 2 Revision 2
sources.versionOrPublished: Editor's Draft 30 December 2025 with linked Web Platform Tests; reviewed 2026-07-15

claims.statement: Adjoining vertical margins in block formatting can collapse across sibling, parent-child, and empty-box relationships. Mixed positive and negative margins use defined arithmetic; a collapsed margin is not universally the larger written value.
limitations: The Editor's Draft is work in progress. Margin behavior also depends on formatting context, clearance, borders, padding, height, min-height, line boxes, writing mode, and newer layout modules, so one positive-margin diagram is not a complete model.

triggers: CSSWG changes adjoining-margin definitions, collapse arithmetic, formatting-context interactions, CSS 2.2 status, errata, or tests.

sources.title: CSS Overflow Module Level 3
sources.versionOrPublished: Current Editor's Draft and 7 October 2025 Working Draft changes reviewed 2026-07-15

claims.statement: Overflow values distinguish visible rendering, hidden clipping with programmatic scrolling, non-scrollable clipping, always available scrolling, and conditional scrolling across axes. Scroll containers affect formatting, focus reachability, scrollbar behavior, and static-media output.
limitations: The Editor's Draft includes recent changes. Selecting an overflow value does not repair the root size constraint or prove long content, focus targets, zoom, touch, keyboard, print, or nested scrolling remain usable.

triggers: CSSWG changes overflow values, axis interaction, scroll-container behavior, clipping, focus, static media, maturity, or test evidence.

claims.statement: Transforms apply after layout but affect rendered coordinates, client rectangles, scrollable overflow, reference-box percentages, containing blocks, stacking contexts, and composed matrix order. They do not reallocate ordinary-flow space for siblings.
limitations: A technically valid transform does not prove visual and focus order agree, text remains legible, targets remain understandable, motion is safe, transformed overflow is reachable, or the transform is the correct layout mechanism.

triggers: CSSWG changes transform composition, origin, reference boxes, overflow, containing blocks, stacking contexts, hit testing, maturity, or implementation tests.

claims.statement: Filter functions form an ordered image-processing pipeline over the rendered element and descendants, create containing and stacking contexts, can add ink overflow without changing box geometry, and define blur by Gaussian standard deviation rather than box-shadow blur radius.
limitations: Filter validity does not establish readable contrast, semantic state, confidentiality, redaction, acceptable performance, usable forced-color output, or a safe no-filter fallback. Rendered blur never removes underlying text or data.

triggers: Filter Effects changes function grammar, processing color space, order, blur model, geometry, stacking or containing behavior, maturity, or test evidence.

sources.title: CSS Flexible Box Layout Module Level 1
sources.versionOrPublished: W3C Candidate Recommendation Draft 14 October 2025; reviewed 2026-07-15

claims.statement: Flex layout defines container and item formation, writing-mode-relative axes, line wrapping, visual-only reordering, automatic minimum sizes, flex base sizes, positive and negative free-space distribution, constraint freezing, and alignment. Order and reverse directions do not replace correct source order.
limitations: The Candidate Recommendation Draft remains work in progress. Property validity alone does not prove a layout survives long content, localization, intrinsic minimums, zoom, changed item counts, keyboard focus, speech order, print, or unfamiliar component constraints.

triggers: CSSWG changes flex item formation, axes, wrapping, automatic minimums, flex sizing, reordering, accessibility requirements, alignment, maturity, or linked Web Platform Test evidence.

claims.statement: Box alignment distinguishes alignment subjects and containers, positional, baseline, and distributed values, content and self alignment, overflow safety, fallback alignment, and fixed gutters. Which box moves depends on layout mode, axis, writing mode, line count, and available space.
limitations: The Working Draft can change. A centered or evenly spaced screenshot does not establish correct property ownership, a usable overflow fallback, baseline behavior, logical direction, wrapped-line behavior, or accessibility under changed content.

triggers: CSSWG changes alignment subjects, containers, keywords, safe or unsafe overflow, fallback behavior, baseline alignment, content distribution, gap behavior, maturity, or tests.

sources.title: Making Content Usable for People with Cognitive and Learning Disabilities
sources.versionOrPublished: W3C Working Group Note 29 April 2021, current errata reviewed 2026-07-15

claims.statement: Clear hierarchy, grouping, labels, signposts, context, focus support, review, correction, confirmation, and return without data loss reduce barriers in complex and interrupted tasks.
limitations: The Note is informative guidance, not a conformance standard, and its patterns require contextual research with disabled people plus current semantic, interaction, privacy, and security evidence.

triggers: W3C changes COGA structure, grouping, focus, authentication, correction, confirmation, personalization, or user-research guidance.

claims.statement: Long collections need explicit navigation, current-page context, understandable neighbouring destinations, and zoom-aware presentation; automatic infinite scroll creates keyboard and context problems.
limitations: A government design-system component is contextual evidence rather than a universal prescription and does not choose pagination boundaries, data loading, history, focus restoration, or virtualization for every product.

triggers: GOV.UK changes pagination, infinite-scroll, keyboard, zoom, labelling, navigation-landmark, or current-page guidance.

claims.statement: Long forms benefit from logical stages, repeated instructions, identifiable optional steps, adjustable timing, and progress communicated through page titles, headings, text, or appropriate progress structures.
limitations: The tutorial does not define product-specific step boundaries, persistence, branching, review, transaction security, network recovery, or a complete current conformance claim.

triggers: WAI changes multi-page form, progress, page-title, heading, optional-stage, timing, or progress-element guidance.

claims.statement: Current browser-based developer handoff can expose prototypes, layers, measurements, variables, shared components, assets, comments, inspectable values, and version context to implementation teams.
limitations: Product documentation describes one vendor workflow and generated values do not establish semantic HTML, responsive behavior, accessibility, performance, correctness, or acceptance by themselves.

triggers: Sketch changes browser handoff, inspect, assets, variables, components, collaboration, permissions, versions, or platform requirements.

claims.statement: A current open design platform exposes view-only inspection, layers, distances, properties, exportable assets, design tokens, prototypes, comments, and code-oriented handoff in browser workflows.
limitations: Penpot feature documentation does not make exported HTML, SVG, CSS, or measurements production-ready without semantic, responsive, accessibility, performance, and changed-case review.

triggers: Penpot changes inspect mode, measurements, code, assets, tokens, prototypes, permissions, comments, or browser workflows.

claims.statement: Adobe identifies XD as being in maintenance mode, so a 2026 beginner curriculum must not present it as an actively evolving default tool or assess memorized vendor rankings.
limitations: Maintenance mode does not erase existing organizational use, files, support obligations, migration needs, or transferable prototype and handoff concepts.

triggers: Adobe changes XD availability, maintenance status, support, security updates, migration, or access to existing content.

claims.statement: Page structure, menus, images, tables, forms, and carousels require programmatic relationships, text alternatives, keyboard behavior, and contextual evidence beyond visual appearance.
limitations: Tutorial techniques are contextual guidance rather than a complete conformance claim and cannot replace current WCAG review or assistive-technology testing.

triggers: WAI changes tutorials, mapped WCAG techniques, page-structure, image, form, menu, or table guidance.

sources.title: W3C WAI How People with Disabilities Use the Web

claims.statement: People combine varied user settings, input and output methods, assistive technologies, and adaptive strategies; disability labels alone do not predict one tool, behavior, barrier, or accommodation.
limitations: WAI examples are non-exhaustive orientation material, do not represent every person or context, and cannot replace representative user research or observed task evidence.

triggers: WAI changes disability, user-story, assistive-technology, adaptive-strategy, or input and output guidance.

sources.title: W3C WAI Selecting Web Accessibility Evaluation Tools

claims.statement: Evaluation tools assist accessibility review but cannot automatically check every accessibility aspect; results require knowledgeable interpretation and complementary manual and user-task evidence.
limitations: Tool-selection guidance does not establish WCAG conformance, certify one tool, define a complete evaluation methodology, or replace testing with disabled people.

triggers: WAI changes evaluation-tool capabilities, selection criteria, automation limits, or complementary evaluation guidance.

claims.statement: Custom widget semantics, states, properties, focus, and keyboard interaction need complete patterns when native HTML cannot satisfy the user need.
limitations: Patterns are non-normative implementation guidance, do not replace native HTML, and require browser and assistive-technology testing in the actual context.

triggers: WAI changes APG patterns, keyboard interaction, accessible-name, state, property, or native-first guidance.

claims.statement: WAI-ARIA normatively defines roles, states, properties, accessible-name and description relationships, and accessibility-tree exposure while leaving interaction behavior and host-language semantics to complete implementations.
limitations: ARIA does not add keyboard behavior, repair malformed HTML, replace native semantics, guarantee browser and assistive-technology support, or prove task usability.

triggers: W3C publishes a superseding WAI-ARIA Recommendation or applicable normative correction.

sources.title: MDN HTML Images and Media Assets

claims.statement: Responsible media use requires verifying ownership, permission, public-domain status, or license conditions before using an asset and preserving required attribution and use evidence.
limitations: MDN provides web-development guidance rather than legal advice and cannot determine rights, exceptions, jurisdiction, or contract terms for a specific asset.

claims.statement: Images and other replaced media obtain their represented content and intrinsic dimensions from external resources rather than ordinary child content in the document tree.
limitations: The learning module provides an author model and does not replace the detailed HTML and CSS specifications for every replaced element, sizing algorithm, resource failure, or privacy behavior.

triggers: MDN changes media ownership, license, attribution, public-domain, search, optimization, or asset-use guidance.

sources.title: U.S. Copyright Office Fair Use Guidance

claims.statement: Fair use is a context-dependent legal doctrine evaluated through four statutory factors and cannot be reduced to a universal educational, noncommercial, credit, percentage, or transformation shortcut.
limitations: U.S. guidance is jurisdiction-specific, only a federal court can conclusively determine fair use, and the course must not give individualized legal advice.

triggers: The Copyright Office changes its fair-use guidance or the course adds jurisdiction-specific rights claims.

sources.title: Creative Commons License Conditions and Types

claims.statement: Creative Commons reuse depends on the exact license and its attribution, ShareAlike, NonCommercial, or NoDerivatives conditions, while CC0 is a separate public-domain dedication tool.
limitations: Summary pages are not substitutes for applicable legal code, rights verification, jurisdictional advice, or checking whether the licensor controlled the offered rights.

triggers: Creative Commons changes license guidance, conditions, attribution practice, public-domain tools, or official license versions.

claims.statement: Responsive Web Design v9 provides the required external topic and practice-depth benchmark across lectures, workshops, labs, reviews, quizzes, projects, and examination.
limitations: Names, counts, and source curriculum do not prove quality and cannot be copied as learner-facing prose, scenarios, code, assessments, or solutions.

triggers: freeCodeCamp changes v9 objectives, blocks, challenge inventory, projects, or examination structure.

sources.title: Direct inspection of the first 61 Responsive Web Design v9 learning units
sources.versionOrPublished: Pinned commit c115efdd41f868d8850156f6a7a211219c35a847, seven opening blocks

claims.statement: The current opening workshop names HTML and the editor, introduces element anatomy in its second unit, and connects source edits to preview output before broader documents, attributes, and debugging work.
limitations: Direct source inspection establishes sequence behavior, not comparative learning effectiveness, accessibility, assessment validity, retention, or successful beginner transfer.

triggers: freeCodeCamp changes the opening workshop, first seven block order, challenge identities, descriptions, starters, or checks.

sources.title: Direct inspection of Responsive Web Design v9 Design for Developers
sources.versionOrPublished: Pinned commit c115efdd41f868d8850156f6a7a211219c35a847, five blocks and 23 challenges

claims.statement: The pinned design module covers visual terminology, contrast, hierarchy, scale, alignment, whitespace, images, progressive enhancement, user research, themes, wayfinding, cards, long collections, dialogs, progress, carts, disclosure, registration, briefs, tools, review, and 40 question prompts.
limitations: Direct inspection establishes benchmark scope and defects, not technical authority, current tool suitability, instructional quality, assessment validity, learner transfer, accessibility, or permission to copy learner-facing material.

triggers: freeCodeCamp changes any Design for Developers challenge, source hash, prompt, example, review, quiz, tool claim, or source-block order.

sources.title: Direct inspection of Responsive Web Design v9 Absolute and Relative Units
sources.versionOrPublished: Pinned commit c115efdd41f868d8850156f6a7a211219c35a847, four blocks and eight challenges

claims.statement: The pinned module contains five unit lectures, an event-flyer lab with 17 checks, a review, and a 20-item quiz covering absolute units, percentages, em and rem, viewport units, auto sizing, and calc arithmetic.
limitations: Direct inspection establishes benchmark scope and defects, not technical authority, responsive behavior, accessibility, assessment validity, retention, transfer, or permission to copy learner-facing material.

triggers: freeCodeCamp changes an Absolute and Relative Units challenge, source hash, check, prompt, solution, review, quiz, or source-block order.

sources.title: Direct inspection of Responsive Web Design v9 Pseudo-classes and Pseudo-elements
sources.versionOrPublished: Pinned commit c115efdd41f868d8850156f6a7a211219c35a847, six blocks and 74 challenges

claims.statement: The pinned module contains seven lectures, two guided workshops, one lab with 20 checks, a review, and two 20-item quiz pools covering interaction, form, location, structural, and functional pseudo-classes plus generated presentation.
limitations: Direct inspection establishes benchmark scope and defects, not technical authority, selector interoperability, accessible interaction, assessment validity, retention, transfer, or permission to copy learner-facing material.

triggers: freeCodeCamp changes a Pseudo-classes and Pseudo-elements challenge, source hash, selector example, check, prompt, solution, review, quiz, or source-block order.

sources.title: Direct inspection of Responsive Web Design v9 CSS Colors
sources.versionOrPublished: Pinned commit c115efdd41f868d8850156f6a7a211219c35a847, five blocks and 98 challenges

claims.statement: The pinned module contains six theory challenges, an 89-step colored-marker workshop, a colored-box lab with 11 checks, a review, and two 20-item quiz pools covering color theory, named, RGB, HSL, and hex notation, alpha, gradients, borders, and shadows.
limitations: Direct inspection establishes benchmark scope and defects, not technical authority, color accuracy, accessibility, instructional quality, assessment validity, retention, transfer, or permission to copy learner-facing material.

triggers: freeCodeCamp changes a CSS Colors challenge, source hash, notation, workshop step, check, prompt, solution, review, quiz, or source-block order.

sources.title: Direct inspection of Responsive Web Design v9 CSS Flexbox and playing-cards lab
sources.versionOrPublished: Pinned commit c115efdd41f868d8850156f6a7a211219c35a847, seven blocks and 71 challenges

claims.statement: The pinned Flexbox family contains two theory challenges, 65 guided workshop steps, a pricing lab with 27 checks, a reference-only review, two 20-item quiz pools, and a playing-cards transfer lab with 18 checks. It contacts container and item formation, axes, direction, wrapping, alignment, gaps, flex sizing, and visual ordering.
limitations: Direct inspection establishes benchmark scope and defects, not technical authority, responsive behavior, accessible order, instructional sufficiency, assessment validity, retained competence, transfer, originality permission, or learner success.

triggers: freeCodeCamp changes a Flexbox or playing-cards challenge, source hash, technical claim, workshop step, check, solution, review, quiz, or source-block order.

sources.title: CodeMirror 6 Documentation, Tab Handling, and Changelog
sources.versionOrPublished: CodeMirror 6 package set current through @codemirror/view 6.43.6; official docs and changelog reviewed 2026-07-15

claims.statement: CodeMirror 6 provides composable browser editor state, views, language modes, commands, autocomplete, lint, accessible content attributes, and intentional no-keyboard-trap Tab behavior; current releases include touch, mobile composition, selection, screen-reader announcement, and diagnostic-navigation repairs.
limitations: Official documentation and release notes do not prove this integration works on the supported device, browser, keyboard, touch, zoom, forced-color, or assistive-technology matrix; observed learner and adversarial tests remain required.

triggers: CodeMirror changes editor state, view, language packages, accessible content attributes, Tab behavior, browser input, touch, composition, or diagnostic behavior.

decisions.title: Teach flex layout through constraint prediction and accessible changed cases
decisions.rationale: Memorizing display flex, alignment keywords, or one shorthand does not explain which box owns a property or how content and constraints determine the result. Learners must reason from source order, writing-mode-relative axes, line formation, flex base and automatic minimum sizes, positive and negative free space, constrained items, alignment subjects, distributable space, gaps, and accessible source, speech, and focus order. Real component behavior under changed content is the mastery evidence.
validation: Activities must make learners predict and inspect axes, line formation, base sizes, automatic minimums, grow and scaled-shrink results, alignment subjects, distributable space, gaps, and source versus visual versus speech versus focus order across changed writing mode, label length, item count, viewport, zoom, and missing-content cases.

Independent flex work must begin from stakeholder behavior constraints, avoid prescribed declaration strings, pass narrow and wide changed cases, preserve meaningful DOM and focus order, and include a causal defense of flex rather than grid or normal flow.

decisions.title: Teach boxes, overflow, transforms, and filters as connected rendering models
decisions.rationale: Literal widths and visual effects do not establish layout understanding. Learners must distinguish box areas and sizing edges, adjoining-margin arithmetic, intrinsic constraints, clipping from scrolling, post-layout transforms, reference boxes, overflow and stacking effects, ordered filter pipelines, rendered contrast, and underlying data. A visual blur is never confidentiality or redaction.
validation: Activities must calculate and inspect box edges, operate changed content and overflow, compare margin groups and formatting contexts, predict transform and filter order, inspect reference boxes, client and overflow geometry, stacking and containing behavior, preserve focus and contrast, expose underlying blurred data, and prove a causal repair on changed cases.

decisions.title: Preserve the complete native form-control contract before custom paint
decisions.rationale: A styled screenshot or appearance-none checkbox does not prove a form works. Learners must preserve labels, instructions, names and values, submission, autocomplete, validation, error recovery, focus, keyboard, touch, speech, zoom, forced colors, and platform affordances. Native controls and accent-color are the default; custom painting is advanced work that must reproduce and test the entire observable contract.
validation: Form activities must submit and restore meaningful data, expose labels and errors, preserve focus and control identity, operate with keyboard, pointer, touch, speech, autofill, zoom, changed content, disabled and read-only states, increased contrast, and forced colors, and prove recovery on changed invalid cases.

decisions.title: Teach color as syntax, processing, output, and accessibility evidence
decisions.rationale: Memorized swatches and notation-shaped checks do not establish color understanding. Learners must distinguish authored syntax, color space, conversion, interpolation, gamut mapping, compositing, computed output, adjacent rendered colors, user color overrides, and non-color meaning, then prove changed behavior instead of copying a palette recipe.
validation: Color activities must operate changed backgrounds, alpha layers, interpolation spaces, output gamuts, light and dark schemes, forced colors, text and component states, and non-color cues while requiring prediction, computed evidence, measurement, causal repair, and design defense.

decisions.title: Separate CSS syntax, selector, cascade, display, and paint models
decisions.rationale: A current CSS curriculum must teach distinct language models from their controlling modules and explicitly correct benchmark shortcuts: rule parsing is not selector matching, specificity is not a decimal or four-place score, important declarations still participate in origin order, display has outer and inner behavior without changing HTML semantics, and backgrounds and borders paint defined box areas.
validation: Concept anchors, explanations, predictions, diagnostics, and changed-case checks must preserve the separate spec models and reject the identified benchmark shortcuts before CSS content can be approved.

decisions.title: Teach units through reference frames and changed cases
decisions.rationale: Unit labels and one-viewport arithmetic are not responsive-design evidence. Learners must identify the property grammar and percentage basis, distinguish CSS reference pixels from device and physical pixels, trace font-relative values, choose among large, small, and dynamic viewport reference frames, validate calc type algebra, and prove content remains available under changed size, content, zoom, and text settings.
validation: Unit activities must include definite and indefinite percentage bases, changed root and local font sizes, zoom and text-spacing changes, small and large viewport UI states, overflow checks, valid and invalid calc expressions, and causal repair explanations before approval.

decisions.title: Use current standards as technical authority
decisions.rationale: External curricula establish coverage expectations, while WHATWG and W3C standards control technical claims and MDN helps translate essential professional outcomes.
validation: Every taught technical rule must trace to a reviewed authority and pass current-browser changed-case tests where behavior matters.

decisions.title: Require media-rights and license evidence
decisions.rationale: Responsible web projects must treat asset ownership, permission, public-domain status, exact license conditions, attribution, and unresolved rights as verifiable design constraints instead of assuming search availability or educational use grants permission.
validation: Media activities and projects must retain asset identity, source, ownership or permission basis, exact license and version, required attribution, allowed use or adaptation, uncertainty, and a refusal path when rights cannot be verified.

decisions.title: Research user needs before visual solutions
decisions.rationale: Responsive interface design must begin from observed user outcomes, constraints, barriers, and task evidence; prototypes and visual decisions remain hypotheses until representative users complete tasks and findings trace into revisions.
validation: Design activities must distinguish evidence, assumptions, needs, solution hypotheses, observations, findings, decisions, and re-test results while including users with varied access needs.

decisions.title: Teach interface patterns as conditional behavior contracts
decisions.rationale: Pattern names and attractive examples do not establish usability. Progressive enhancement, themes, breadcrumbs, cards, long collections, dialogs, multi-step flows, carts, and disclosure must be selected only from user and task evidence, implemented with complete semantic and interaction contracts, and rejected when a simpler page structure fits better.
validation: Every pattern activity must require selection or rejection rationale, semantic and state contracts, keyboard and assistive-technology behavior, changed-content and failure cases, recovery, and observed representative-user evidence before approval.

decisions.title: Teach durable handoff capabilities, not stale tool trivia
decisions.rationale: A 2026 developer needs to inspect prototypes, structure, dimensions, variables, components, assets, comments, permissions, and versions across current design workflows, but vendor popularity and memorized product lists decay quickly and generated handoff values never replace implementation judgment.
validation: Learners must transfer a versioned design brief through at least one inspectable browser handoff, identify semantic and responsive unknowns, and implement changed cases without being assessed on vendor rankings or maintenance-only product tutorials.

decisions.title: Build responsive systems from content constraints
decisions.rationale: Fluid defaults, intrinsic sizing, modern layout, content-derived breakpoints, component containers, and changed-case testing produce more defensible responsive behavior than targeting named devices or copying fixed screenshots.
validation: Labs and projects must survive narrow and wide containers, zoom, text spacing, long or translated content, media failure, keyboard use, and relevant user preferences without device-name breakpoints.

decisions.title: Match v9 coverage with original learning work
decisions.rationale: Certification breadth and thousands of small interactions set the minimum depth target, but every explanation, scenario, artifact, check, project, and solution must be original and meaningfully varied.
validation: Granular objective mapping, semantic duplication review, human lesson inspection, and beginner pilots must pass without token-count shortcuts.

decisions.title: Teach HTML through immediate bounded construction
decisions.rationale: True beginners need explicit syntax and mental models tied to immediate editing; long abstract tooling sequences must not delay creating and inspecting their first HTML structure.
validation: Opening learner flow must explain elements, tags, content, nesting, attributes, and documents while meaningful editing begins within two workshop steps.

decisions.title: Teach complete beginner tooling after first HTML evidence
decisions.rationale: Pinned v9 and current beginner authorities require computer, input, account, file, browser, internet, search, and developer-tool competence, but these skills should enter after the learner edits HTML and at the first artifact decision where each capability becomes useful.
validation: Opening flow must preserve meaningful HTML editing by action two, then verify all twelve tooling competencies through reversible simulations, authentic transfer boundaries, keyboard access, security constraints, and later retrieval.

decisions.title: Keep accessibility active in every later build
decisions.rationale: Semantic structure, keyboard use, naming, status, reflow, contrast, input, preferences, and assistive-technology evidence are continuing correctness constraints, not one isolated module.
validation: Later CSS, responsive, animation, lab, project, and exam work must retain and re-test previously introduced accessibility competencies.

decisions.title: Grade parsed and observed web behavior
decisions.rationale: Source substrings cannot establish document relationships, accessible names, rendered behavior, responsive adaptation, or survival under changed content and preferences.
validation: Checks must parse structure, inspect computed behavior, exercise changed cases, and reject malformed, misplaced, inaccessible, or deleted retained work.

openQuestions: All 158 current freeCodeCamp v9 block identities and 1,553 challenge identities are mapped, but their concept-level standards, misconception, prerequisite, and pedagogical meaning still require review beyond inventory parity.
HTML beginner sequence needs expert and novice review before generator expansion.
CSS module order needs dependency review against current specifications and beginner misconceptions.
Five certification project briefs need originality, independence, accessibility, and rubric-validity review.
The CodeMirror 6 editor flow needs observed keyboard, screen-reader, zoom, touch, composition, recovery, and real-tablet testing.
Sandbox, diagnostics, canonical checks, changed-case execution, persistence, reset, and checkpoint boundaries need threat and failure review.

triggers: Referenced standards, MDN curriculum, freeCodeCamp v9 inventory, browser support, CodeMirror support, WCAG, or learner evidence changes.
